2.2.4. Determination of Solubility Parameters of the Nanoparticles Components
Solubility parameters of the solvent blends were calculated and used for comparison to
study the correlation with the nanoparticle formation in this study. The solubility parameter

column1: was introduced by Hildebrand and Scott to determine an estimation of the drug/excipient

column2: miscibility [29]. Hildebrand deﬁned the solubility parameter, δT, as square root of the
cohesive energy density, EP, of a substance. This is the energy required to separate or attract

column1: the atoms or molecules from each other.
where ∆Hv is the heat of vaporization, R is the gas constant, T is the temperature and the

use is limited for non-polar solvents. Derivatives approximations of Hildebrand’s equation
have been described to better establish the interactions between substances. One of these
is the Hansen approach [30]. HaSP is derived from three different solvent energies: ED
measured as the intermolecular dispersion energy, EP as the dipolar intermolecular energy,
and EH as the hydrogen bonding energy. The sum of the square energies divided by the
